draft.introduction: Proposition 15 of Proclus’s Elements of Theology (5th century CE) establishes that everything capable of reverting upon itself is incorporeal. This principle became fundamental to the Neoplatonic understanding of the soul’s capacity for self-knowledge and return to the divine.

translation.text: Everything capable of reverting upon itself is incorporeal. For no body is capable of reverting upon itself. That which reverts upon itself must be united with itself as a whole; but no body can be united with itself as a whole, since every body is divisible and has its parts in different places. Therefore no body is capable of reverting upon itself.
translation.original_text: πᾶν τὸ πρὸς ἑαυτὸ ἐπιστρεπτικὸν ἀσώματόν ἐστιν

translator_notes.note: The concept of epistrophe (ἐπιστροφή), reversion or return, is central to Proclus’s system: all things proceed from the One and revert toward it. Self-reflection is the first step in this return. The capacity of the soul to know itself demonstrates its incorporeality and its kinship with the divine.
